prose: Shall I, in my contemplation, truly compare thee to the fleeting beauty of a summer's day?
poem: Shall I compare thee to a summer's day?

prose: For thou art more lovely and more temperate than any transient thing nature can offer:
poem: Thou art more lovely and more temperate:

prose: The rough winds do shake and disturb the darling buds of May with indifferent force,
poem: Rough winds do shake the darling buds of May,

prose: And summer's lease upon this world hath all too short a date, ending before we are prepared;
poem: And summer's lease hath all too short a date;

prose: Sometime too hot and unrelenting the eye of heaven shines down upon us,
poem: Sometime too hot the eye of heaven shines,

prose: And often is his gold complexion dimmed by the obscuring veil of clouds;
poem: And often is his gold complexion dimmed;

prose: And every fair thing from its fair essence sometime declines into shadow,
poem: And every fair from fair sometime declines,

prose: By chance or by nature's changing course left untrimmed and unguided;
poem: By chance or nature's changing course untrimmed;

prose: But thy eternal summer, unlike all others, shall not fade into oblivion,
poem: But thy eternal summer shall not fade,

prose: Nor shall thy being lose possession of that fair beauty thou ow'st to existence;
poem: Nor lose possession of that fair thou ow'st;

prose: Nor shall death brag that thou wander'st helplessly in his cold shade,
poem: Nor shall death brag thou wander'st in his shade,

prose: When in these eternal lines of verse thou grow'st bound to time itself:
poem: When in eternal lines to time thou grow'st:

prose: So long as men can breathe the air of life or eyes can see the world,
poem: So long as men can breathe or eyes can see,

prose: So long lives this verse, and this enduring verse gives life to thee.
poem: So long lives this, and this gives life to thee.

prose: Remember me, I implore thee, when I am gone away from this mortal world,
poem: Remember me when I am gone away,

prose: Gone far away into the silent land of eternal stillness and absence;
poem: Gone far away into the silent land;

prose: When you can no more hold me by the hand as you once did,
poem: When you can no more hold me by the hand,

prose: Nor I half turn to go yet turning stay suspended between departure and presence.
poem: Nor I half turn to go yet turning stay.

prose: Remember me when no more day by day the rhythm of life brings us together
poem: Remember me when no more day by day

prose: And you tell me of our future and the life together that you plann'd:
poem: You tell me of our future that you plann'd:

prose: Only remember me in your quiet moments; you understand the weight of this
poem: Only remember me; you understand

prose: It will be late and futile to counsel then or pray for what cannot return.
poem: It will be late to counsel then or pray.

prose: Yet if you should forget me for a while in the necessary current of living
poem: Yet if you should forget me for a while

prose: And afterwards remember my existence, do not grieve with unnecessary sorrow:
poem: And afterwards remember, do not grieve:

prose: For if the darkness and corruption of time leave behind
poem: For if the darkness and corruption leave

prose: A vestige, however faint, of the thoughts that once I had in living,
poem: A vestige of the thoughts that once I had,

prose: Better by far that you should forget and smile at the world
poem: Better by far you should forget and smile

prose: Than that you should remember my absence and be sad with prolonged mourning.
poem: Than that you should remember and be sad.

prose: Let me not stand as obstacle to the marriage of true minds
poem: Let me not to the marriage of true minds

prose: And admit impediments to their union; for love is not love
poem: Admit impediments; love is not love

prose: Which alters its nature when it alteration finds in circumstance,
poem: Which alters when it alteration finds,

prose: Or bends with the remover, yielding, to remove itself from difficulty.
poem: Or bends with the remover to remove.

prose: O no, it is an ever-fixèd mark standing in the cosmos
poem: O no, it is an ever-fixèd mark

prose: That looks on tempests and the violence of existence and is never shaken;
poem: That looks on tempests and is never shaken;

prose: It is the guiding star to every wandering bark adrift at sea
poem: It is the star to every wandering bark

prose: Whose worth's unknown to human measure, although his height be taken by instruments.
poem: Whose worth's unknown, although his height be taken.

prose: Love's not time's fool nor its servant, though rosy lips and cheeks
poem: Love's not time's fool, though rosy lips and cheeks

prose: Must within his bending sickle's compass come to inevitable decay.
poem: Within his bending sickle's compass come.

prose: Love alters not its fundamental nature with his brief hours and weeks,
poem: Love alters not with his brief hours and weeks,

prose: But bears it out with endurance even to the very edge of doom:
poem: But bears it out even to the edge of doom:

prose: If this be error in my understanding and upon me proved by truth,
poem: If this be error and upon me proved,

prose: Then I never writ a word of meaning, nor no man ever truly loved.
poem: I never writ, nor no man ever loved.

prose: When I see birches bend to left and right under the weight of existence
poem: When I see birches bend to left and right

prose: Across the lines of straighter darker trees that stand without yielding,
poem: Across the lines of straighter darker trees,

prose: I like to think some boy's been swinging them in innocent abandon.
poem: I like to think some boy's been swinging them.

prose: But swinging doesn't bend them down to stay in permanent submission
poem: But swinging doesn't bend them down to stay

prose: As ice-storms do with cold authority. Often you must have seen them
poem: As ice-storms do. Often you must have seen them

prose: Loaded with ice on a sunny winter morning, burdened yet illuminated,
poem: Loaded with ice a sunny winter morning

prose: After a rain has passed. They click upon themselves in hollow percussion
poem: After a rain. They click upon themselves

prose: As the breeze rises with quiet insistence, and turn many-colored
poem: As the breeze rises, and turn many-colored

prose: As the stir of wind cracks and crazes their enamel with inevitable force.
poem: As the stir cracks and crazes their enamel.

prose: Soon the sun's warmth, that indifferent force, makes them shed crystal shells
poem: Soon the sun's warmth makes them shed crystal shells

prose: Shattering and avalanching in cascades on the snow-crust below—
poem: Shattering and avalanching on the snow-crust—

prose: Such heaps of broken glass and ruin to sweep away from the earth
poem: Such heaps of broken glass to sweep away

prose: You'd think the inner dome of heaven itself had fallen to the ground.
poem: You'd think the inner dome of heaven had fallen.

prose: They are dragged down to the withered bracken by the load of what they carry,
poem: They are dragged to the withered bracken by the load,

prose: And they seem not to break under pressure; though once they are bowed
poem: And they seem not to break; though once they are bowed

prose: So low for so long, they never right themselves to former grace:
poem: So low for long, they never right themselves:

prose: You may see their trunks arching in quiet resignation in the woods
poem: You may see their trunks arching in the woods

prose: Years afterwards, still trailing their leaves on the ground in surrender
poem: Years afterwards, trailing their leaves on the ground

prose: Like girls on hands and knees that throw their hair forward
poem: Like girls on hands and knees that throw their hair

prose: Before them over their heads to dry in the indifferent warmth of the sun.
poem: Before them over their heads to dry in the sun.

prose: But I was going to say something deeper when Truth broke in
poem: But I was going to say when Truth broke in

prose: With all her matter-of-fact authority about the ice-storm
poem: With all her matter-of-fact about the ice-storm

prose: I should prefer to have some boy bend them through the will of play
poem: I should prefer to have some boy bend them

prose: As he went out and in through the rhythms of labor to fetch the cows—
poem: As he went out and in to fetch the cows—

prose: Some boy too far from town and its conventions to learn baseball,
poem: Some boy too far from town to learn baseball,

prose: Whose only play was what he found himself in solitude,
poem: Whose only play was what he found himself,

prose: Summer or winter alike, and could play alone without need of others.
poem: Summer or winter, and could play alone.

prose: One by one he subdued his father's trees through patient persistence
poem: One by one he subdued his father's trees

prose: By riding them down over and over again in repeated acts of will
poem: By riding them down over and over again

prose: Until he took the stiffness out of them through determined effort,
poem: Until he took the stiffness out of them,

prose: And not one but hung limp in surrender, not one was left standing
poem: And not one but hung limp, not one was left

prose: For him to conquer. He learned all there was to know
poem: For him to conquer. He learned all there was

prose: To learn about not launching out too soon into the unknown
poem: To learn about not launching out too soon

prose: And so not carrying the tree away beyond the point of return
poem: And so not carrying the tree away

prose: Clear to the ground. He always kept his poise with deliberate grace
poem: Clear to the ground. He always kept his poise

prose: To the very top branches, climbing carefully with philosophical patience
poem: To the top branches, climbing carefully

prose: With the same pains and deliberate attention you use to fill a cup
poem: With the same pains you use to fill a cup

prose: Up to the brim of its capacity, and even above the brim.
poem: Up to the brim, and even above the brim.

prose: Then he flung outward into the void, feet first, with a swish,
poem: Then he flung outward, feet first, with a swish,

prose: Kicking his way down through the resistant air to the waiting ground.
poem: Kicking his way down through the air to the ground.

prose: So was I once myself a swinger of birches in younger days.
poem: So was I once myself a swinger of birches.

prose: And so I dream of going back to be that free spirit once more.
poem: And so I dream of going back to be.

prose: It's when I'm weary of endless considerations and their burden,
poem: It's when I'm weary of considerations,

prose: And life is too much like a pathless wood without direction or meaning
poem: And life is too much like a pathless wood

prose: Where your face burns and tickles with the invisible cobwebs
poem: Where your face burns and tickles with the cobwebs

prose: Broken across it by passage, and one eye is weeping involuntarily
poem: Broken across it, and one eye is weeping

prose: From a twig's having lashed across it open without warning.
poem: From a twig's having lashed across it open.

prose: I'd like to get away from earth awhile and its accumulated weight
poem: I'd like to get away from earth awhile

prose: And then come back to it renewed and begin over with clarity.
poem: And then come back to it and begin over.

prose: May no fate willfully misunderstand me in its cold administration
poem: May no fate willfully misunderstand me

prose: And half grant what I wish yet snatch me away before completion
poem: And half grant what I wish and snatch me away

prose: Not to return. Earth's the right place for love and its unfolding:
poem: Not to return. Earth's the right place for love:

prose: I don't know where it's likely to go better than here on earth.
poem: I don't know where it's likely to go better.

prose: I'd like to go by climbing a birch tree toward understanding,
poem: I'd like to go by climbing a birch tree,

prose: And climb black branches reaching up a snow-white trunk toward light
poem: And climb black branches up a snow-white trunk

prose: Toward heaven and its abstraction, till the tree could bear no more weight,
poem: Toward heaven, till the tree could bear no more,

prose: But dipped its top in gentle arc and set me down again on earth.
poem: But dipped its top and set me down again.

prose: That would be good and sufficient both going upward and coming back.
poem: That would be good both going and coming back.

prose: One could do considerably worse in this life than be a swinger of birches.
poem: One could do worse than be a swinger of birches.

prose: Something fundamental there is in existence that doesn't love a wall,
poem: Something there is that doesn't love a wall,

prose: That sends the frozen-ground-swell of resistance under it,
poem: That sends the frozen-ground-swell under it,

prose: And spills the upper boulders out in the open sun;
poem: And spills the upper boulders in the sun;

prose: And makes gaps in the structure even two can pass abreast through.
poem: And makes gaps even two can pass abreast.

prose: The work of hunters in their pursuit is another thing entirely:
poem: The work of hunters is another thing:

prose: I have come after them in their wake and made repair to the damage
poem: I have come after them and made repair

prose: Where they have left not one stone resting on a stone,
poem: Where they have left not one stone on a stone,

prose: But they would have the rabbit forced out of hiding,
poem: But they would have the rabbit out of hiding,

prose: To please the yelping dogs of appetite. The gaps I mean,
poem: To please the yelping dogs. The gaps I mean,

prose: No one has seen them made or heard them made by any hand,
poem: No one has seen them made or heard them made,

prose: But at spring mending-time we find them there waiting.
poem: But at spring mending-time we find them there.

prose: I let my neighbor know of this beyond the hill;
poem: I let my neighbor know beyond the hill;

prose: And on a day appointed we meet to walk the line together
poem: And on a day we meet to walk the line

prose: And set the wall between us once again in its proper place.
poem: And set the wall between us once again.

prose: We keep the wall between us as a necessary division as we go.
poem: We keep the wall between us as we go.

prose: To each of us the boulders that have fallen to each by circumstance.
poem: To each the boulders that have fallen to each.

prose: And some are loaves in their shape and some so nearly balls
poem: And some are loaves and some so nearly balls

prose: We have to use a kind of spell and careful art to make them balance:
poem: We have to use a spell to make them balance:

prose: We say: 'Stay where you are in your place until our backs are turned!'
poem: 'Stay where you are until our backs are turned!'

prose: We wear our fingers rough and worn with the labor of handling them.
poem: We wear our fingers rough with handling them.

prose: Oh, it is just another kind of out-door game we play,
poem: Oh, just another kind of out-door game,

prose: One on a side in our division. It comes to little more than ritual:
poem: One on a side. It comes to little more:

prose: There where it is between us we do not need the wall:
poem: There where it is we do not need the wall:

prose: He is all pine in his nature and I am apple orchard in mine.
poem: He is all pine and I am apple orchard.

prose: My apple trees will never get across the boundary between us
poem: My apple trees will never get across

prose: And eat the cones lying under his pines, I tell him plainly.
poem: And eat the cones under his pines, I tell him.

prose: He only says without reflection, 'Good fences make good neighbors.'
poem: He only says, 'Good fences make good neighbors.'

prose: Spring is the mischief and questioning spirit in me, and I wonder
poem: Spring is the mischief in me, and I wonder

prose: If I could put a deeper notion in his unquestioning head:
poem: If I could put a notion in his head:

prose: 'Why do they make good neighbors in truth? Isn't it only
poem: 'Why do they make good neighbors? Isn't it

prose: Where there are cows to contain? But here there are no cows.
poem: Where there are cows? But here there are no cows.

prose: Before I built a wall I'd ask to know with certainty
poem: Before I built a wall I'd ask to know

prose: What I was walling in or walling out from my existence,
poem: What I was walling in or walling out,

prose: And to whom I was like to give offense by such an act.
poem: And to whom I was like to give offense.

prose: Something essential there is in the nature of things that doesn't love a wall,
poem: Something there is that doesn't love a wall,

prose: That wants it down into ruin.' I could say 'Elves' to him as explanation,
poem: That wants it down.' I could say 'Elves' to him,

prose: But it's not elves exactly that I mean, and I'd rather
poem: But it's not elves exactly, and I'd rather

prose: He said it for himself through his own reflection. I see him there
poem: He said it for himself. I see him there

prose: Bringing a stone grasped firmly and deliberately by the top
poem: Bringing a stone grasped firmly by the top

prose: In each hand, like an old-stone savage armed against the unknown.
poem: In each hand, like an old-stone savage armed.

prose: He moves in darkness and unknowing as it seems to me,
poem: He moves in darkness as it seems to me,

prose: Not of woods only and the mere shade of trees.
poem: Not of woods only and the shade of trees.

prose: He will not go behind his father's saying to question its foundation,
poem: He will not go behind his father's saying,

prose: And he likes having thought of it so well in his certainty
poem: And he likes having thought of it so well

prose: He says again with satisfaction, 'Good fences make good neighbors.'
poem: He says again, 'Good fences make good neighbors.'

prose: I have been one deeply acquainted with the night and its philosophy.
poem: I have been one acquainted with the night.

prose: I have walked out in rain and returned back in rain without shelter.
poem: I have walked out in rain—and back in rain.

prose: I have outwalked even the furthest city light into deeper darkness.
poem: I have outwalked the furthest city light.

prose: I have looked down the saddest city lane with unflinching contemplation.
poem: I have looked down the saddest city lane.

prose: I have passed by the watchman standing on his beat in the darkness
poem: I have passed by the watchman on his beat

prose: And dropped my eyes downward, unwilling to explain my wandering.
poem: And dropped my eyes, unwilling to explain.

prose: I have stood still in the darkness and stopped the sound of feet
poem: I have stood still and stopped the sound of feet

prose: When far away in the distance an interrupted cry of existence
poem: When far away an interrupted cry

prose: Came over the houses from another street in the night,
poem: Came over houses from another street,

prose: But not to call me back to warmth or say good-bye to sorrow;
poem: But not to call me back or say good-bye;

prose: And further still above at an unearthly height of abstraction,
poem: And further still at an unearthly height,

prose: One luminary clock standing against the indifferent sky
poem: One luminary clock against the sky

prose: Proclaimed with authority that the time was neither wrong nor right.
poem: Proclaimed the time was neither wrong nor right.

prose: I have been one who is deeply acquainted with the night and its silences.
poem: I have been one acquainted with the night.
